summary: My first inspiration to dance came when I saw a performance in Melton Mowbray. That experience really sparked my passion for ballet and led me to start dancing classes. It was the beginning of a lifelong journey in dance.

Queenie Green was one of my early influences in dance. She helped ignite my enthusiasm and love for ballet during my formative years. Her impact stayed with me as I progressed in my training and career.

During World War II, I performed on military bases and balanced my dance studies with the challenges of wartime life. Food rationing made things tough, but I managed to continue private lessons with Mary Skeaping and perform with Lydia Kyasht's company. The war years were difficult but formative for my dancing.

I studied for The Royal Academy of Dance examinations during the war, alongside Cecchetti exams. This formal training was crucial in developing my technique and discipline as a dancer. The Academy's syllabus gave me a strong foundation in ballet.

Mary Skeaping was one of my private teachers during the war. Her lessons were invaluable, especially given the difficulties of that time. She helped me maintain and improve my skills despite the challenges of wartime.

I had the opportunity to study and perform with Lydia Kyasht's company during World War II. This experience enriched my performance skills and exposed me to professional company work early on. It was an important part of my development as a dancer.

When I moved near London at fifteen, I took classes at Sadler's Wells Theatre and observed company performances there. It was an inspiring environment that deepened my understanding of ballet. The theatre was central to my early professional experiences.

I auditioned for the Sadler's Wells Ballet and was closely involved with the company through the Sadler's Wells School. Although a knee injury limited my performing, I taught the junior associates group connected to the company. I also witnessed choreographers like Frederick Ashton creating works for Sadler's Wells Ballet.

I attended the Sadler's Wells School after moving to London and later taught there. The school grew significantly during my time, adding uniforms, an education teacher, and expanding its syllabus. I was involved in developing the level system and helped shape the school's curriculum alongside Ninette de Valois.

While training near London, I took classes at The New Theatre, which complemented my studies at Sadler's Wells. This venue was part of my early dance education and exposure to professional ballet environments.

Ninette de Valois played a pivotal role in my career, inviting me to teach ballet in Turkey and involving me in developing the Sadler's Wells syllabus. I worked closely with her on forming the day school and the Sadler's Wells Theatre Ballet. She was a guiding figure throughout my professional life.

I had the privilege of observing Frederick Ashton choreograph works for the Sadler's Wells Ballet. Watching his creative process was inspiring and educational. His artistry influenced my understanding of ballet choreography and performance.

Kathleen Hills was the bursar at Sadler's Wells School and later became my mother-in-law. She was involved in the school's administration during its period of growth and change. Our connection extended beyond professional to family life.
Brian was someone we met during the time when Sadler's Wells School was expanding and television was becoming common. His presence is a small but memorable part of that era in the school's history.

I was asked by Ninette de Valois to teach ballet in Turkey for a year, which was a unique and challenging experience. We had to adapt to cultural differences, learn Turkish, and create a ballet syllabus from scratch. It was rewarding to lay the foundation for ballet education there.

While in Turkey, I lived in Ankara and taught ballet to children alongside Joy Newton. Living there required adjusting to local customs and protocols, including strict rules about public behavior. Ankara was the center of my teaching work during that year abroad.

Joy Newton was my colleague in Ankara, Turkey, where we taught ballet together. We faced many challenges, including language barriers and sourcing ballet supplies. Working with her made the experience more manageable and fulfilling.

After I left Turkey, Beatrice Appleyard took over the ballet school there. She continued the work we had started, ensuring the foundation we laid was built upon. Her role was important in sustaining ballet education in Turkey.

Molly Lake was one of the teachers who followed Beatrice Appleyard in running the ballet school in Turkey. Along with Travis Kemp, she helped maintain and develop the program after I returned to England.

Travis Kemp was involved with Molly Lake in continuing the ballet school in Turkey after my departure. Their work helped keep the program alive and growing. I felt confident leaving the school in their capable hands.

Naomi Capon created a documentary about the Sadler's Wells School, capturing its history and development. This film helped preserve the legacy of the school and the work we did there. It was an important recognition of our efforts.
